content.text: The mechanics of running an eval — iterating over test cases, calling the model, scoring outputs, and storing results — are repetitive enough that a dedicated framework pays off quickly. Understanding what these frameworks provide (and what they do not) lets you choose the right tool for your task and build a simpler custom runner when you do not need one.

attrs.alt: Eval framework architecture and tool comparison

content.text: The Four Components Every Eval Runner Needs
Regardless of which framework or tool you use, a functioning eval runner always has four responsibilities:
Read cases from disk (JSONL, CSV, or a database), validate their schema, and expose them as an iterable. A missing or malformed case should fail loudly, not silently produce a zero score.

The bottleneck in any eval is latency, not CPU. Sending cases to the model one at a time wastes 90% of available wall-clock time. The runner must issue calls concurrently, respecting your API rate limit.
For each (input, output) pair, select and invoke the right grader — exact match, regex, execution-based, or LLM judge — and return a scalar or structured score.
Write the score, suite hash, model identifier, and timestamp to a durable store so that trends can be tracked across runs and deployments.
A custom runner is the right choice when your suite is small (under a few hundred cases), your grading logic is straightforward, and you want zero new dependencies.

import asyncio, json, hashlib, time
from pathlib import Path
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

client = AsyncAnthropic()

async def call_model(case: dict, model: str, semaphore: asyncio.Semaphore) -> dict:
    async with semaphore:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=case.get("system_prompt", ""),
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        return {**case, "output": response.content[0].text}

def grade(result: dict) -> float:
    expected = result.get("expected", "")
    output = result["output"].strip()
    if result.get("grader") == "exact":
        return 1.0 if output.lower() == expected.lower() else 0.0
    if result.get("grader") == "contains":
        return 1.0 if expected.lower() in output.lower() else 0.0
    return 0.0  # unknown grader defaults to fail

async def run_eval(suite_path: str, model: str, concurrency: int = 10) -> dict:
    cases = [json.loads(l) for l in Path(suite_path).read_text().splitlines() if l.strip()]
    suite_hash = hashlib.sha256(json.dumps([c["id"] for c in sorted(cases, key=lambda c: c["id"])]).encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

    semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[call_model(c, model, semaphore) for c in cases])
    graded = [grade(r) for r in results]
    score = sum(graded) / len(graded)

    run_result = {
        "suite": suite_path,
        "suite_hash": suite_hash,
        "model": model,
        "score": round(score, 4),
        "n": len(cases),
        "timestamp": time.time(),
    }
    # Append to results log
    with open("eval_results.jsonl", "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(run_result) + "\n")
    return run_result

# asyncio.run(run_eval("suites/my_suite.jsonl", "claude-opus-4-8"))
The semaphore limits concurrent requests. Tune the concurrency parameter to stay below your API tier's requests-per-minute limit — a value of 10-20 is safe for most tiers without adding retry logic.
Inspect is the evaluation framework released by the UK AI Safety Institute. It is purpose-built for capability evaluations and provides the runner, grader, and log-viewer as a single install:

from inspect_ai import Task, eval
from inspect_ai.dataset import json_dataset
from inspect_ai.solver import generate
from inspect_ai.scorer import exact

# Define a task — the unit of evaluation in Inspect
my_task = Task(
    dataset=json_dataset("suite.jsonl"),
    solver=generate(),          # call the model
    scorer=exact(),             # exact-match grader
)

# Run with Claude
results = eval(my_task, model="anthropic/claude-opus-4-8", limit=100)
print(results[0].metrics)

Inspect's architecture separates Solvers (how the model is called and prompted), Scorers (how outputs are graded), and Tasks (the combination of dataset + solver + scorer). This separation makes it easy to swap model providers or graders without rewriting evaluation logic.
For more complex grading, Inspect provides an LLM scorer that issues a judge call and a model_graded_qa scorer for Q&A tasks:

from inspect_ai.scorer import model_graded_qa

fact_task = Task(
    dataset=json_dataset("factual_qa.jsonl"),
    solver=generate(),
    scorer=model_graded_qa(
        model="anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        template="Grade whether ANSWER correctly answers QUESTION. Score 1 for correct, 0 for incorrect.",
    ),
)

Braintrust is a hosted platform that adds dataset management, experiment comparison, and human annotation on top of the eval runner. It is the right choice when multiple team members review results, when you want to track experiments across prompt versions, or when human labeling is part of the grading pipeline.

import braintrust
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

@braintrust.traced
def run_case(input_text: str, system: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def grader(output: str, expected: str) -> braintrust.Score:
    correct = expected.strip().lower() in output.strip().lower()
    return braintrust.Score(name="contains_expected", score=1 if correct else 0)

# Run an experiment — results appear in the Braintrust web UI
with braintrust.init(project="my-llm-app", experiment="claude-opus-4-8-v1") as experiment:
    for case in load_cases("suite.jsonl"):
        output = run_case(case["input"], case.get("system", ""))
        experiment.log(
            input=case["input"],
            output=output,
            expected=case["expected"],
            scores={"contains_expected": grader(output, case["expected"]).score},
        )

The Braintrust web UI shows each experiment as a row in a comparison table. When a new run regresses on a case that passed before, the UI highlights the delta with a diff view — which makes root-cause analysis faster than comparing raw JSONL files.

Weights & Biases (W&B) and LangSmith are primarily tracing and experiment-tracking platforms that include eval support as a feature. They are the best choice when your stack already uses these tools for model training (W&B) or LangChain-based pipelines (LangSmith).
Neither tool requires you to use the full platform. You can use LangSmith's evaluator API as a standalone grader by passing input/output pairs directly:

from langsmith import Client
from langsmith.evaluation import evaluate

ls_client = Client()

def anthropic_target(inputs: dict) -> dict:
    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": inputs["question"]}],
    )
    return {"answer": response.content[0].text}

results = evaluate(
    anthropic_target,
    data="my-dataset",           # dataset name in LangSmith
    evaluators=["correctness"],  # built-in LLM judge
    experiment_prefix="claude-opus-4-8",
)

Start with a custom runner. Switch to a framework when you feel the absence of a specific feature it provides — not before. Every framework adds a dependency, an abstraction, and a learning curve; the value must justify each.

An eval that only runs manually is not a reliability signal — it is a one-off check. Wire the runner into your CI pipeline so that every pull request runs the eval suite against the current model and blocks merge on regression.

# .github/workflows/eval.yml
name: Eval gate
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic
      - run: python eval_gate.py --suite suites/core.jsonl --model claude-opus-4-8 --threshold 0.90
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

# eval_gate.py
import asyncio, sys, argparse
from runner import run_eval  # your custom runner or framework wrapper

async def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--suite")
    parser.add_argument("--model")
    parser.add_argument("--threshold", type=float, default=0.90)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    result = await run_eval(args.suite, args.model)
    print(f"Score: {result['score']:.3f}  (threshold: {args.threshold})")
    if result["score"] < args.threshold:
        print("FAIL: regression detected")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("PASS")

asyncio.run(main())
Keep the CI suite small — under 200 cases — so that runs complete in under two minutes. A slow eval gate gets disabled or bypassed. Run the full suite on a nightly schedule, not on every commit.
Eval results are only useful if you can compare them across time. Store each run in a structured format and query it programmatically.

import sqlite3, json

def init_results_db(path: str = "eval_runs.db") -> sqlite3.Connection:
    conn = sqlite3.connect(path)
    conn.execute("""
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS runs (
            id        INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
            suite     TEXT,
            suite_hash TEXT,
            model     TEXT,
            score     REAL,
            n         INTEGER,
            timestamp REAL
        )
    """)
    conn.execute("""
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS case_results (
            run_id    INTEGER REFERENCES runs(id),
            case_id   TEXT,
            score     REAL,
            output    TEXT
        )
    """)
    conn.commit()
    return conn

def detect_regression(conn: sqlite3.Connection, model: str, threshold: float = 0.02) -> bool:
    rows = conn.execute(
        "SELECT score FROM runs WHERE model=? AND suite_hash=? ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT 2",
        (model, "latest_hash")
    ).fetchall()
    if len(rows) < 2:
        return False
    latest, previous = rows[0][0], rows[1][0]
    return (previous - latest) > threshold

Eval frameworks reduce boilerplate and provide parallelism, reproducibility, and result tracking. The right choice depends on team size, tooling ecosystem, and whether human annotation is part of your grading pipeline.
A custom async runner covers most small-to-medium suites with no additional dependencies
Inspect is the best open-source choice for capability evaluations that need a CLI and log viewer
Braintrust and LangSmith add collaboration, human annotation, and experiment comparison for teams
All runners must persist suite hashes alongside scores to enable valid run-over-run comparisons
Integrate the eval runner into CI as a gate; keep CI suites under 200 cases for speed
